coredata.dc:title: Impact of solvent on the downconversion efficiency of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer: Application in CIGS solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Firoz
Alshahrani, Thamraa
Fareed, Imran
Al-Rasheidi, Masoud
Ahmad, Nafis
Al-Ahmed, Amir
Zahir, Md. Hasan
Kim, Jae Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Due to lowering the material cost, the thin film-based photovoltaic (PV) technologies are progressively drawing substantial concentration in the PV community. Among them, Cu(In, Ga)Se2 (CIGS) is considered a potential absorbing material due to its higher absorption and changeable band-gap. But it produced a low value of current at the short wavelength region. Thus, the short circuit current density (Jsc) is greatly reduced, which results in a lower value of the conversion efficiency. Here, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are used as luminescent photon downshifters (LDS). Furthermore, the N-GQDs are embedded in the poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) to enhance the chemical and mechanical stability of the LDS. However, the PMMA creates the photoluminescence (PL) quenching, which lessens the efficacy of the LDS layer. The LDS composite layer is formed in the various solvents. Furthermore, these LDS composites were evaluated via deployment onto the CIGS solar cells. The best performance is obtained in the chlorobenzene solvent. By insertion of the LDS composite layer, the Jsc was enhanced from 33.58 to 35.32 mA/cm2, which resulted in an enhancement in the efficiency from 13.08% to 14.62%. The PV cell parameters analysis revealed that recombination is reduced due to growing the number of photons, which results in the performance enhancement of the CIGS solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CIGS solar cell
N-doped graphene quantum dots
PMMA matrix
Downconverters

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman and (b) FTIR spectra of N-GQDs.
(a) Absorption and (b) PL spectra at λex = 450 nm of N-GQDs.

(a) Optical image of the N-GQDs/PMMA coated layer on the quart’s substrate under illumination of UV-radiation (λex = 365 nm) and (b) PL emission spectra at λex = 405 nm (in inset: TEM image of N-GQDs/PMMA layer).
Schematic of the downconversion mechanism in the N-GQDs/PMMA layer.
(a) Illuminated J-V curves of CIGS cell with LDS along with bare cell and (b) EQE of bare and Chlb cells.
Variation of (a) Jsc and (b) Voc values before and after insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer prepared in various solvent.
Variation of (a) FF and (b) η values before and after insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer prepared in various solvent.
Extraction of slopes of illuminated J-V characteristics at (a) the open circuit and (b) short circuit conditions.
Variation in the values of Jph, Rsh, and Rs values before and after insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer prepared in various solvent.
Variation in the values of n and J0 values before and after insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer prepared in various solvent.
The values of performance parameters of the CIGS solar cells.
The values of performance parameters of the CIGS solar cells.

head.ce:title: Impact of solvent on the downconversion efficiency of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer: Application in CIGS solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Convergence Research Group, Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science & Technology (DGIST), 333, Techno Jungang-Daero, Hyeonpung-Myeon, Dalseong-Gun, Daegu-42988, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to lowering the material cost, the thin film-based photovoltaic (PV) technologies are progressively drawing substantial concentration in the PV community. Among them, Cu(In, Ga)Se2 (CIGS) is considered a potential absorbing material due to its higher absorption and changeable band-gap. But it produced a low value of current at the short wavelength region. Thus, the short circuit current density (Jsc) is greatly reduced, which results in a lower value of the conversion efficiency. Here, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are used as luminescent photon downshifters (LDS). Furthermore, the N-GQDs are embedded in the poly(methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) to enhance the chemical and mechanical stability of the LDS. However, the PMMA creates the photoluminescence (PL) quenching, which lessens the efficacy of the LDS layer. The LDS composite layer is formed in the various solvents. Furthermore, these LDS composites were evaluated via deployment onto the CIGS solar cells. The best performance is obtained in the chlorobenzene solvent. By insertion of the LDS composite layer, the Jsc was enhanced from 33.58 to 35.32 mA/cm2, which resulted in an enhancement in the efficiency from 13.08% to 14.62%. The PV cell parameters analysis revealed that recombination is reduced due to growing the number of photons, which results in the performance enhancement of the CIGS solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Photovoltaic (PV) technologies are considered as clean energy sources, which convert sunlight into electrical energy. Sun is an abundant source of energy, which is unpolluted and free. Thus, PV technologies are used to transform solar energy into electrical energy. The PV technologies are classified into four generations on basis of time and absorbing materials [1]. The thin-film technology belongs to the second generation. The purpose of the second-generation solar cells is to considerably cut down the materials cost of the first-generation solar cells [1]. Thus, copper indium gallium diselenide (CIGS) solar cells (CIGS-SCs) are believed as a potential aspirant for low-cost PV technology. Recently, 23.35% efficiency (η) was achieved in the Cd-free CIGS solar cell [2,3]. However, the high η of the terrestrial CIGS-SCs is constrained by the small spectral response in the short wavelength (λ)-range (300–450 nm) [4]. This low spectral response is caused by the parasitic absorption in the ZnO/CdS layers [5,6]. Consequently, there is a great chance to advance the η of the CIGS-SCs to reach the Shockley–Queisser limit (~33%) [7,8]. To achieve efficiency close to the theoretical limit, well-organized photon management is needed via next-generation conceptions, such that photon downshifting (DS), downconversion (DC), and upconversion (UC) [9]. For this purpose, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10–12], organic dye [13,14], rare earth complex [15–17], inorganic quantum dots [18–21], and are used. However, these LDS are not appropriate for the CIGS-SCs either due to small photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) or a short Stokes-shift [22]. In this regard, efficient nitrogen-doped graphene QDs (N-GQDs) are developed, which offered ultra-high PLQY (~99%) and large Stokes-shift [8,23]. However, for the improvement of the chemical, mechanical and thermal stabilities, encapsulation is required. Previously, for the encapsulation of the rare-earth complex, numerous polymer matrixes, such that [poly(ethylene-vinyl acetate) (EVA) [24,25], poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) [26,27], poly(vinyl acetate) (PVA) [9], or acryl resins [28] have been used. The PLQY and Stokes-shift of the N-GQDs strongly depend on the solvent used for the synthesis of the N-GQDs/polymer composite layer. Thus, it is important to investigate the impact of the solvent on the DC/DS efficacy of the N-GQDs/polymer composite.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized as reported elsewhere [29,30]. An aqueous solution of concentration 2 mg/ml was of GO was prepared followed by functionalization of GO using polyethyleneimine (PEI). The mixture solution was stirred at 90 °C for 4 h to get GO/PEI composite. Then the functionalized GO was heat-treated in a quartz furnace under inert ambient at 300 °C for 1 h. The obtained paste was sonicated in the various solvents to obtain the N-GQDs solutions. The N-GQDs samples with the solvent’s ethanol, chlorobenzene, and dimethylformamide are simply referred to as EtOH, Chlb, and DMF, respectively. The concentration of the N-GQDs in the corresponding solvents was 2 mg/ml. Furthermore, 20 mg of PMMA was dispersed in 1 ml of N-GQDs solution. The N-GQDs/PMMA layer was coated on the quartz glass. The layers were dried at 60 °C for 2 h. Hereafter, the N-GQD/PMMA DC layer covered CIGS-SCs will be mentioned as CIGS-EtOH, CIGS-Chlb, and CIGS-DMF for the ethanol, chlorobenzene, and DMF solvents used for N-GQDs dispersion. For the reference cell, bare quartz glass is put on top of the CIGS-SCs, which will be referred as “Bare” cell.

The PL spectra were acquired at an excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 405/450 nm using a PL spectrophotometer (Darsa, PSI Trading Co. Ltd.) with a Xe lamp. A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscope (Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to obtain FTIR spectra under the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The absorbance spectra of the N-GQDs were obtained using a UV–vis–near-infrared spectrophotometer (Carry-5000, Agilent Technologies). The Raman spectra were recorded using a confocal Raman spectrometer (Nicolet Almeca XRA, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with λ ex of 532 nm. Surface analyses were conducted using nonconducting atomic force microscopy (AFM; Park Systems, NX10). A high-resolution image of N-GQDs/PMMA was obtained using transmittance electron microscopy (TEM, HF-3300/NB5000/S-4800, Hitachi).

CIGS-SCs were first fabricated as described in the previous report [31]. In brief, molybdenum (Mo)-coated soda-lime glass (SLG) was properly cleaned via sonication in acetone. Approximately ~3 µm CIGS layer was deposited on the Mo/glass via a three-stage co-evaporation method [31]. Then CdS buffer layer (approximately 50 nm) was deposited on the CIGS layer through the chemical bath deposition (CBD) technique [32]. A window layer was deposited using intrinsic ZnO (i-ZnO) and Al-doped ZnO (ZnO:Al) (approximately 50 and 350 nm, respectively) were coated by DC magnetron sputtering. Furthermore, Ni/Al top contact was created by the e-beam evaporation technique. The J–V characteristics (active area of ~0.43 cm2) was obtained at an illumination intensity of 100 mW/cm2 of AM 1.5 G using a source meter (Keithley 2400). A Xe arc lamp-based solar simulator system (Newport, 91192) is used to illuminate the solar cell. The PV measurement system was used to obtain external quantum efficiencies (EQEs) of CIGS-SCs (PV Measurement Incorporated, G1218a).

The analytical PV cell parameters are extracted using an analytical method [33], based on a single diode model. This method is requiring Jsc, Voc, current density (Jm), and voltage (Vm) at maximum power point along with the inverse of the slopes (ISLP, (dJ/dV)−1) at short circuit (SC) and open (OC) circuit conditions [34,35]. The ISLP values at SC and OC are represented by Rsc and Roc, respectively.

The ISLP at OC (V = 0, J = Jsc) gives the value Rsh as given by Eq. (1).
(1)

sc

The value of n can be predicted by Eq. (2).
(2)

The value of Rs can be predicted by Eq. (3).
(3)

The value of J0 can be predicted by Eq. (4).

where V

= kT/q. k is Boltzmann’s constant, T is temperature, and q is elementary charge [36].

The size and height of the N-GQDs were estimated via the AFM topography. The obtained height of the N-GQDs is 3−5 nm ( Fig. 1). These N-GQDs are highly functionalized with the N-related functional group, thus the height is slightly higher than our previously reported N-GQDs [23]. As the number of the attached functional group to the graphitic structure of N-GQDs is increased, the dimension is enlarged.

The Raman and FTIR studies were conducted for further confirmation of the structural and bonding properties of the N-GQDs. Fig. 2a displays the Raman spectra of the N-GQDs. Two main peaks are seen at 1262.1 and 1585.4 cm−1, which are related to the D and G peaks of the graphitic structure of N-GQDs, respectively [29,37]. The D peak designates the imperfections in the graphitic structure. Nevertheless, the G peak signifies the graphitic nature of the N-GQDs, which is related to the dual-degenerated E2 g mode of the hexagonal lattice of the N-GQDs [38]. Additionally, one peak corresponding to N-moieties is observed at 1399.1 cm−1, which confirms the doping of N-related functional groups. Thus, highly N-doped GQDs are synthesized. The FTIR spectra of N-GQDs revealed that two absorbance bands corresponding to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations are observed at 3405.6 and 3248.3 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2b) [39,40]. Three absorbance peaks C-H stretching vibration are detected at 2944.8, 2861.8, and 1091.7 cm−1 [41]. An intense peak is seen at 1645.2 cm−1, which is due to the bond signature of CC bonding presence in the N-GQDs graphitic structure. Moreover, numerous absorption peaks associated with CC/C-N bonding are seen in the wavenumber range of 1460.6–1276.1 cm−1. This observation confirms the existence of N-GQDs [39].

The extensive absorption in the λ-range of 300–500 nm is extremely important for the effectiveness of the LDS material suitable for CIGS-SCs. Fig. 3a illustrates the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the N-GQDs. A wide-ranging absorption is observed for the N-GQDs due to the grouping of various individual absorption bands [8]. These individual absorption bands are situated at λ = 223, 278, 315, and 353 nm, which results in an extensive absorption band (λ ~ 200 – 500 nm). The absorption peaks at λ = 223 nm are due to the π→π*transition of CC (of sp2 C domain in sp3 C matrix). Nevertheless, the transition of π→π * CO or CN is instigated to originate the absorption bands at λ = 278, 315, and 353 nm. Fig. 3b illustrates the PL spectra of the N-GQDs. The PL emission peak is found at 529.5 nm for the λex = 450 nm, which delivers a Stokes-shift of 80.5 nm. The emission peaks position should be higher than 500 nm for the efficient utilization of the LDS material in the CIGS solar cells. Thus, these N-GQDs are useful as an LDS layer for the CIGS solar cells.

PMMA was added to the N-GQDs prepared in ethanol, chlorobenzene, and DMF to make a composite solution. These composite solutions were used to spin coat layers of N-GQDs/PMMA onto a quartz substrate. Then the N-GQDs/PMMA layers are dried under N2 ambient. Fig. 4a demonstrates an illumination optical image of the N-GQDs/PMMA film prepared using the N-GQD/PMMA solution in chlorobenzene via excitation of UV light of wavelength 365 nm. Besides, an image of bare quartz glass is additionally displayed for comparison. The cyan color of the N-GQDs/PMMA film indicates the wavelength of the emitted photons would be ~490 nm. Furthermore, the PL emission spectra of the N-GQDs/PMMA films were acquired at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm (Fig. 4b). The PL peak position for all the samples is obtained at λ = ~500 nm, which can be a suitable LDS layer for the CIGS-SCs. The PL intensity of the N-GQDs/PMMA prepared in chlorobenzene is highest; however, the lowest intensity is obtained in ethanol solvent. These results revealed that all the N-GQDs/PMMA films produced a large Stokes-shift (~95 nm). The TEM image of N-GQDs/PMMA in chlorobenzene solvent is shown in the inset of Fig. 4b, which revealed that the N-GQDs are consistently dispersed in the PMMA. Fig. 5 shows the schematic of the downconversion mechanism in the N-GQDs/PMMA system. In the CIGS solar cells, most of the short-λ photons (< 500 nm) are absorbed in the top layers (i.e., ZnO and CdS), which are not fully contributed to the current generation. When the CIGS solar cell with N-GQDs/PMMA is illuminated with light, the photons of wavelength > 500 nm are directly reached to the CIGS absorbing layer. Thus, most of the photons in the λ-range of 500 – 1300 nm are directly absorbed in the CIGS, which are contributing to the current generation. While the photons of wavelength < 500 nm are absolved in the N-GQDs/PMMA downconversion (DC) layer, which is re-radiate the photons of appropriate wavelengths that can straightforwardly be absorbed in the CIGS absorbing layer. Thus, the total current is improved, which consequences for the improvement in the η.

The J-V characteristics were obtained for the determination of the performance parameters of CIGS-SCs. Fig. 6a illustrates the J-V characteristics of bare and N-GQD/PMMA CIGS-SCs. The performance parameters of the CIGS-SCs are listed in Table 1. The dependencies of Jsc and Voc values are shown in Fig. 7a and b. The maximum enhancement in the Jsc value is obtained for the CIGS-Chlb cell. The Jsc value obtained for CIGS-Chlb is 35.32 mA/cm2, which is 5.2% higher than the bare cell (33.58 mA/cm2). This enhancement in Jsc is mainly due to the enhancement in the spectral response in the low-λ region, which can be confirmed from the EQE differences between CIGS-Chlb and bare cell (Fig. 6b). However, the Jsc improvement for the EtOH and DMF solvents are 3.1% and 4.3%, respectively. However, the enhancement in the Voc is 0.6%, 2%, and 1% for the CIGS-EtOH, CIGS-Chlb, and CIGS-DMF solar cells, respectively. The variation of the FF is illustrated in Fig. 8a. The FF values obtained for CIGS-EtOH, CIGS-Chlb, and CIGS-DMF are 61.10%, 61.63%, and 61.33%, respectively, which are quite higher than the FF of the bare cell (59.20%). Hence, the overall conversion efficiency is enhanced from 13.08% to 14.00%, 14.62%, and 14.28%, respectively for the CIGS-EtOH, CIGS-Chlb, and CIGS-DMF cells (Fig. 8b). This improvement is expected to be a combined influence of photon management as well as the reduction of optical losses [23].

Fig. 9a and b show the fitted J-V curves at OC and SC conditions. The slope at the OC is increased with the insertion of the DC layer, indicating that series resistance values are reduced due to enhancement in the Jsc [34]. However, the slope at short circuit condition is reduced, which indicates shunt resistance is reduced.

The extracted PV cell parameters are listed in Table 2. Fig. 10a displays the variation of the Jph in various solvents. The Jph value is increased from 33.83 mA/cm2 to 34.83, 35.68, and 35.27 mA/cm2 for the EtOH, Chlb, DMF solvents, respectively. This improvement is due to the rise in the number of photons absorbed in the CIGS absorbing layer, which enhanced the Jsc value. The highest gain of 5.5% in Jph is obtained for the Chbl solvent. Fig. 10b illustrates the changes in the Rsh value. The Rsh value is also increased with the insertion of the DC layer, however, the greatest improvement of Rsh value (from 190.48 to 247 Ωcm2) is observed for the Chlb. High photon absorption in Chlb cells results in high charge carrier generation. These charges filled the number of the traps, which result in a low Rsh value [34]. The value of Rs changes is illustrated in Fig. 10c. The series resistance of the EtOH is slightly reduced from 1.39 to 1.29 Ωcm2, however, an enhancement is observed for Chlb and DMF.

The changes in the n and J0 are illustrated in Fig. 11a and b, respectively. The n value is reduced from 2.6223 to 2.4598, 1.7573, and 2.3639 for EtOH, Chlb, and DMF, respectively. Similarly, the J0 value is reduced from 1.7315 × 10−3 mA/cm2 to 8.9343 × 10−4, 1.139 × 10−5, and 5.7220 × 10-4 mA/cm2. The highest reduction is obtained for the Chlb solvent.

Highly efficient N-GQDs were synthesized in three solvents. Then PMMA was added to each N-GQDs solution to form N-GQDs/PMMA composite. The N-GQDs/PMMA layers were synthesized via spin coating. The highest PL intensity was obtained for the N-GQDs/PMMA layer formed in chlorobenzene solvent. By applying N-GQDs/PMMA layer to CIGS solar cells, the J-V characteristics were measured. The best solar cell performance is also obtained for the N-GQDs/PMMA LDS for the chlorobenzene solvent. By insertion of the LDS composite layer, the short circuit current density was enhanced from 33.58 to 35.32 mA/cm2, which resulted in an increase in η from 13.08% to 14.62%. Furthermore, the PV cell parameters were inspected to explore the losses analysis. The values of n and J0 are reduced via insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer. The lowest values of n and J0 are obtained for the CIGS-Chlb cell (n = 1.7573 and J0 = 1.139 ×10−5 mA/cm2).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Energy Technology Development Program of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP, Grant No. 20203030010200), South Korea. The authors also express their gratitude to Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R1), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of Nanomaterials for Industrial Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. Hussain, Handbook of nanomaterials for industrial applications, Elsevier, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cd-free Cu (In, Ga) (Se, S) 2 thin-film solar cell with record efficiency of 23.35%

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nakamura, K. Yamaguchi, Y. Kimoto, Y. Yasaki, T. Kato, H. Sugimoto, Cd-free Cu (In, Ga)(Se, S) 2 thin-film solar cell with record efficiency of 23.35%, IEEE Journal of Photovoltaics, 9 (2019) 1863–1867.
M. Green, E. Dunlop, J. Hohl‐Ebinger, M. Yoshita, N. Kopidakis, X. Hao, Solar cell efficiency tables (version 57), Progress in photovoltaics: research and applications, 29 (2021) 3–15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Friedlmeier, Thin‐film solar cells exceeding 22% solar cell efficiency: An overview on CdTe-, Cu (In, Ga) Se2-, and perovskite-based materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Powalla, S. Paetel, E. Ahlswede, R. Wuerz, C.D. Wessendorf, T. Magorian Friedlmeier, Thin‐film solar cells exceeding 22% solar cell efficiency: An overview on CdTe-, Cu (In, Ga) Se2-, and perovskite-based materials, Applied Physics Reviews, 5 (2018) 041602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: What limits the efficiency of chalcopyrite solar cells?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Siebentritt, What limits the efficiency of chalcopyrite solar cells?, Sol. Energy Mater. Sol. Cells, 95 (2011) 1471–1476.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thin Film Solar Cells: Fabrication, Characterization and Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Poortmans, V. Arkhipov, Thin film solar cells: fabrication, characterization and applications, John Wiley & Sons, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detailed balance limit of efficiency of p‐n junction solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Shockley, H.J. Queisser, Detailed balance limit of efficiency of p‐n junction solar cells, J. Appl. Phys., 32 (1961) 510–519.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emission-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence decay lifetime of N-functionalized graphene quantum dot downconverters: Impact on conversion efficiency of Cu (In, Ga) Se 2 solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Khan, J.H. Kim, Emission-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence decay lifetime of N-functionalized graphene quantum dot downconverters: Impact on conversion efficiency of Cu (In, Ga) Se 2 solar cells, Scientific reports, 9 (2019) 1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving spectral response of monocrystalline silicon photovoltaic modules using high efficient luminescent down‐shifting Eu3+ complexes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, K. Wang, W. Zheng, W. Huang, C.H. Li, X.Z. You, Improving spectral response of monocrystalline silicon photovoltaic modules using high efficient luminescent down‐shifting Eu3+ complexes, Progress in Photovoltaics: Research and Applications, 21 (2013) 668–675.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dot layers with energy-down-shift effect on crystalline-silicon solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.D. Lee, M.J. Park, D.-Y. Kim, S.M. Kim, B. Kang, S. Kim, H. Kim, H.-S. Lee, Y. Kang, S.S. Yoon, Graphene quantum dot layers with energy-down-shift effect on crystalline-silicon solar cells, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 7 (2015) 19043–19049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficiency enhancement of silicon heterojunction solar cells via photon management using graphene quantum dot as downconverters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.-L. Tsai, W.-C. Tu, L. Tang, T.-C. Wei, W.-R. Wei, S.P. Lau, L.-J. Chen, J.-H. He, Efficiency enhancement of silicon heterojunction solar cells via photon management using graphene quantum dot as downconverters, Nano Lett., 16 (2016) 309–313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra‐high quantum yield of graphene quantum dots: aromatic‐nitrogen doping and photoluminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sun, S. Yang, Z. Wang, H. Shen, T. Xu, L. Sun, H. Li, W. Chen, X. Jiang, G. Ding, Ultra‐high quantum yield of graphene quantum dots: aromatic‐nitrogen doping and photoluminescence mechanism, Particle & Particle Systems Characterization, 32 (2015) 434–440.
